summary: Mary Midgley was a British philosopher who argued forcefully against scientism and Cartesian dualism, insisting that humans are fundamentally animals and part of the complex, living system of Earth (Gaia). She viewed philosophy as a vital human need akin to 'plumbing'—a practice of fixing broken or leaky concepts—and advocated for using imagination, poetry, and an understanding of our biological and social nature to navigate moral and environmental crises.

principles.statement: We are not just like animals we are animals.
principles.rationale: Human nature is comprised of motivational tendencies (like seeking attachment, playing, or dominating) that we share with other creatures. Morality and values stem directly from what we need as social, culture-creating animals.
principles.source_quote: we are not just like animals we are animals

principles.statement: The climate crisis is fundamentally a conceptual emergency.
principles.rationale: We lack the right metaphysical picture of our place in nature and our own vulnerability. Instead of focusing on transhumanist ambitions to escape Earth, we need new concepts and visions for how to live in a mixed community with other animals.

principles.statement: Reality is manifoldly complex, and contradictions are natural.
principles.rationale: No single discipline can explain everything. We need the special sciences, social sciences, and humanities to map different aspects of a highly complex reality.
principles.source_quote: Conflict, contradiction, complexity. You know, that's just our lot. And that's the kind of reality that we're trying to navigate.

frameworks.name: Philosophical Plumbing
frameworks.when_to_apply: When societal thought stagnates, or when people cannot make their experiences intelligible due to troublesome or outdated concepts.
steps: Look at the world to identify where there are conceptual dysfunctions or blockages in thought at the current historical moment.
Avoid trying to create lists of necessary and sufficient conditions in isolation (the armchair method).
Use forensic analysis to dig deep and locate the structural leak or blockage in the concept.
Employ the skills of the poet—metaphor, analogy, and play—to envision new conceptual frameworks and widen perspective.

mental_models.name: Philosophy as Plumbing
mental_models.description: Concepts are not simple, neat categories; they are complex, historical structures like the plumbing under an old medieval city. Over time, they accumulate additions, blockages, and leaks. Philosophers are the plumbers who must go in, find the blockages causing thought to stagnate, and repair the system.
mental_models.example: Exploding the dichotomy between 'man' and 'beast' to fix the leaky concept that humans are somehow separate from the animal kingdom.

mental_models.name: The Gaia Hypothesis
mental_models.description: A lens for viewing the Earth not as a dead resource, but as an incredibly complex, self-regulating living system that we depend on. It scales up our understanding of ourselves from isolated, selfish genes to social, relational, and vulnerable members of a planetary community.
mental_models.example: Using Gaia as an antidote to the atomistic, egoistic picture of humanity promoted by the 'selfish gene' narrative.

heuristics: When people get stuck by being obsessed with a single way of looking at things, stand back and see what the neighbors of this are.
To understand human nature, look to the ethologists (like Jane Goodall) rather than just the physicists.
Use analogy to fling open wide your perspective.

signature_quotes.text: Our relation to the earth is as close as our relation to the people around us on whom we most depend. We naturally love it. It has made us what we are. We are part of it. We're grateful to it and we respect it.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the Gaia hypothesis and our relationship to the environment.

signature_quotes.text: Conflict, contradiction, complexity. You know, that's just our lot. And that's the kind of reality that we're trying to navigate.
signature_quotes.context: Describing her view of reality as a philosopher of complexity.

signature_quotes.text: When people get stuck by being obsessed with a single way of looking at things, stand back and see what the neighbors of this are.
signature_quotes.context: Advising on how to unclog muddled thinking.

signature_quotes.text: The trouble with this sort of talk, what would one call scientism, it distracts people from thinking about their own lives in the direct way that they can.
signature_quotes.context: Critiquing the belief that science can explain absolutely everything.

anti_patterns.description: Scientism: The belief that science can explain absolutely everything and that fundamental physical or biological entities provide the bedrock explanation for all reality.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It distracts people from thinking about their own lives directly, ignores the complexity of reality, and inappropriately applies simple scientific models to complex social and moral domains.

anti_patterns.description: The Cartesian Disembodied Mind: Doing philosophy as if humans are isolated, timeless, disembodied minds (like Descartes doubting the world from his room).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores our embodied, social, and animal nature. If philosophers had different embodied experiences (like being pregnant or breastfeeding), they would not arrive at extreme external world skepticism.

anti_patterns.description: The 'Selfish Gene' Rhetoric: Using simple, scientific-sounding metaphors like 'selfishness' to describe fundamental biological nature.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It distorts our self-understanding by ignoring our inherent sociality and interdependence. Selfishness only makes sense as a concept when contrasted with our natural tendency to share and care for one another.
